sections.title: Food Spoilage
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the concept of food spoilage through the story of detective Disha, who finds her forgotten uttapam covered in mould after three days. Her elder brother Aditya explains that microbes — tiny living organisms visible only under a microscope — are responsible for the coloured patches and foul smell, and that they exist everywhere in soil, water, air, plants, animals, and inside the human body. The section further explores why some foods (like bread) spoil quickly while others (like oil-covered pickles) last longer, leading Disha to investigate what conditions microbes need to grow: moisture, air, and the right temperature.

tasks.task_text: Have you ever had an upset stomach? Do you know what could have caused it? What could happen if spoiled food is eaten by mistake? What did you do to get well? Share your experiences with the class.
Why do you think food gets spoiled?

sections.title: Food Preservation
sections.content_summary: This section follows Disha as she observes four traditional and modern food preservation methods at home: drying and dehydration (chillies dried in the sun to make chilli powder, removing moisture so microbes cannot grow), pickling and oiling (mustard oil poured over pickled mangoes to block air), refrigeration and freezing (cold temperature slowing microbial growth), and use of salt, sugar, and spices like pepper — methods used for centuries in India and globally. The section also notes airtight canning and packaging used in food factories, and introduces the role of good microbes in making idli batter rise through fermentation, illustrating that not all microbes are harmful.

tasks.task_text: What other things are dried so that they remain unspoilt throughout the year?
What items can be made from mangoes to enjoy them for longer durations? Write their names in the space given below.

1. Take a tomato and cut it into slices.
2. Put the slices of tomatoes on a tray and place it on the window where the sunlight comes through.
3. What changes did you observe in the fruit?

Can you think of a way to preserve items like tomato?
What would happen to this pickle if no oil had been added?
Find out about food preservation practices at your family by asking the elders at home. Write at least one such practice.

sections.title: My Food, My Pride
sections.content_summary: This section expands Disha's detective diary to explore traditional food practices and the importance of seasonal and local eating. Disha visits the vegetable market with her Thatha (grandfather), observing how elders carefully select fresh produce. The section celebrates seasonal fruits such as lychee, explaining how local and seasonal food is fresher because it travels a shorter distance. It also introduces indigestion and home remedies, noting that curd and buttermilk contain good microbes that support digestion, while bad eating habits can cause indigestion.

tasks.task_text:
Do you ever accompany your elders for buying vegetables and fruits? During such visits, observe how elders select fruits and vegetables in the market.

Write one thing that you learnt from your shopping trip and write it as a Finding #9, the way Disha would write.
Name three seasonal fruits or vegetables of your region. For example, amla in winter and jamun in summer.

sections.title: Chew Right!
sections.content_summary: This section investigates what happens to food inside the mouth, with Disha exploring the role of different teeth (incisors to cut, canines to tear, premolars to crush, and molars to grind) and the function of saliva in digestion. It teaches that chewing food properly breaks it down and releases saliva which aids digestion. The section also covers oral hygiene — including the importance of rinsing the mouth, brushing teeth regularly, and visiting a dentist — and safety around eating: taking smaller mouthfuls, chewing properly, and not gulping food to avoid choking.

tasks.task_text: When you eat, try chewing each item properly till it breaks down into smaller pieces and then into mush. Which teeth did you use to cut and chew? Look at the diagram given on the next page and name them.

sections.title: Food in Our Body
sections.content_summary: The chapter concludes with the final discovery that microbes play a dual role — some spoil food while others help make food (like idli) and aid digestion in the stomach. Disha closes her detective notebook having resolved the mystery: eating right, preserving and storing food correctly, chewing properly, and appreciating the role of good microbes are all key to health and well-being. The Let us reflect questions consolidate understanding of food preservation, and the matching activity links food items, preservation methods, and final products.

tasks.task_text: Why do some food items last for weeks and others spoil in just a few days?
You are going on a field trip for two days. List five food items you will carry. How will you keep them from getting spoiled?
What if food cannot be preserved? Imagine a world without food preservation and discuss the consequences.
Match the following. Which method is used to preserve the following food items (A) to make the final product (B)?

dual_strand.natural: Microbial action, food spoilage, fermentation, and the role of teeth and saliva in digestion
dual_strand.human_cultural: Traditional Indian food preservation practices (drying, pickling, oiling, use of spices), seasonal and local eating traditions, and oral hygiene practices
